Charlie Munger — The Psychology of Human Misjudgment

PATRICK: Charlie, you've talked about mental models for decades. Why do you think most investors still ignore them? CHARLIE: Because it requires genuine intellectual effort and most people are lazy in their thinking. They'd rather use a simple heuristic they already know than learn a dozen new frameworks. The irony is that the mental model approach is actually easier once you've internalized it because you have so many more tools available to diagnose problems. PATRICK: Let's talk about some of your most important models. Where would you start? CHARLIE: I'd start with inversion. Most people ask how to achieve a goal. I ask how to avoid the opposite outcome. If you want to be happy ask yourself what would make you miserable and avoid those things. If you want a successful business ask yourself what would make it fail and avoid those failure modes. Carl Jacobi the mathematician said it best: invert always invert. PATRICK: How about incentives? You talk about that a lot. CHARLIE: Incentives are the most powerful force in human behavior. Show me the incentive and I'll show you the outcome. The pharmaceutical industry produces miracle drugs because the incentive is enormous. But it also produces harmful drugs because the incentive to hide negative data is also enormous. You should never underestimate the power of incentives to corrupt even well-intentioned people. PATRICK: What about your famous lollapalooza effect? CHARLIE: The lollapalooza is when multiple biases and incentives align in the same direction and the combined effect is far greater than the sum of the parts. You see it in cults, in financial bubbles, in marketing. When social proof combines with scarcity and authority and reciprocity you get behavior that seems inexplicable from any single factor but makes complete sense when you look at the combination. PATRICK: How do you think about the role of reading in developing judgment? CHARLIE: Reading is absolutely essential. Warren and I both read voraciously. We read annual reports, history books, biographies, scientific literature. The goal is to build a latticework of mental models that you can apply across domains. Most people stay very narrow in their reading which means they keep encountering the same problems they've already solved and missing new problems that look different on the surface but are structurally identical to something they should know about. PATRICK: What's the biggest mistake you see investors make repeatedly? CHARLIE: The biggest mistake is buying mediocre businesses at cheap prices instead of wonderful businesses at fair prices. This was my own biggest mistake early in my career. Cheap businesses usually stay cheap because they have bad economics. The Graham style of deep value investing worked brilliantly in an era of inefficient markets but the market has gotten much more efficient at spotting statistically cheap securities. PATRICK: If you were starting a new investment firm today what would you do differently than Berkshire? CHARLIE: I'd be more willing to pay up for quality. Buffett and I learned this lesson from See's Candies. We paid three times book value which seemed outrageous at the time. But we learned that a wonderful business at a fair price is better than a fair business at a wonderful price. The key insight is that a business with durable competitive advantages compounds your capital year after year without requiring you to do anything. PATRICK: What qualities do you look for in the people you invest in or work with? CHARLIE: Intelligence, energy, and integrity. And the most important of the three is integrity. An intelligent and energetic person without integrity will just destroy your capital more efficiently than a stupid person would. You need all three. I look for people who are genuinely trustworthy, who have demonstrated good judgment under pressure, and who have intellectual curiosity that keeps them learning. PATRICK: Thank you Charlie. This has been an extraordinary conversation. CHARLIE: My pleasure Patrick. Keep reading and keep thinking. The world rewards people who are genuinely thoughtful and genuinely curious far more generously than it rewards people who are merely industrious.
